actions.thought: In a 3-handed game, the button is a powerful position, and I should be opening a wide range of hands to capitalize on this advantage. While Ace-Trey offsuit is not a premium hand, it has sufficient high-card equity to justify an open-raise to steal the blinds. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard and effective sizing to apply pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. The Button has raised to 3x the big blind. Calling out of position with such a poor hand is a losing play in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

We are playing 3-handed. The Button opens to 3bb (300). I hold A9o in the Big Blind. This hand is well ahead of a standard Button opening range, which is typically very wide in a 3-handed game. I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450) to defend my blind. Although playing out of position is a disadvantage, A9o has enough raw equity and top-pair potential to justify a call. 3-betting is an option, but A9o plays acceptably as a call and 3-betting risks getting blown off by a 4-bet or dominated when called by better Aces. I will flat call to see the flop.

I defended the Big Blind with A9o and the flop comes Q-8-2 rainbow. I missed the flop completely, holding only Ace high and no draws. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard and most optimal line here is to check. Leading out (donking) makes little sense as the Queen connects well with the pre-flop raiser's range, and I have very little equity to continue aggressively. I will check to evaluate my opponent's action.

The flop of Q-8-2 rainbow is relatively dry, and as the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high, I have reasonable equity and showdown value. A small continuation bet of 200 into the 650 pot allows me to represent the Queen and apply pressure on hands like smaller pocket pairs or overcards that missed, while maintaining position control.

The flop is Q-8-2 rainbow, and I missed entirely, holding just Ace-high with no backdoor flush draws. The pre-flop raiser fired a continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 650. Even though the bet size is small and I am getting reasonable odds, I am out of position with very little equity and poor playability on future streets. Calling to float out of position is unprofitable, and I don't have the right hand properties to check-raise bluff. Folding is the best decision.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit (Ac 9s), which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I should open for a standard raise to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. A 3x open to 300 chips is a standard play here.

Holding 3d 8c in the Small Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. This is a very weak hand with no high card strength, suitedness, or connectivity. The only strategically sound decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

Holding 2s8d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability. Calling an open raise from the button while playing out of position is negative expected value. Folding is the standard disciplined play to preserve my stack.

With a small pocket pair like 3s 3d on the Button in a 3-handed game, open-raising is a standard play. It allows me to steal the blinds outright or play a heads-up pot in position. I'll raise to 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the small blind with 6-5 offsuit, a very weak hand. Playing this hand out of position is negative expectation, especially since the big blind has yet to act. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 3h, which is an extremely weak hand. The button (Pro) raised to 2.5x the big blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 550), playing 73o out of position against an aggressive player is not profitable. I have no equity or playability with this hand and should fold to preserve my stack.

Ks 3h is a weak hand with poor connectivity and a low kicker. Playing this hand, especially when facing a requirement to call 100, is statistically unfavorable. I choose to fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c6c. After the Button folds, it's a heads-up situation between me and the Big Blind (Pro). In blind-vs-blind play, 63s is strong enough to open for a raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot down immediately. Given that I successfully stole the blinds in Hand #2, maintaining an aggressive posture here is consistent with a winning strategy, even if the hand is at the bottom of my raising range.
I have T7o in the big blind facing a 3x raise from the small blind. This hand is too weak, unsuited, and disconnected to defend, even with position. Folding is the standard and optimal play here.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Standard play is to open for a raise to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the blinds. A 3x raise to 300 has been effective in previous hands and represents a strong range.

J4 suited is a very weak hand, especially facing a 3x open raise from the Button. Being out of position in the Small Blind against the pre-flop raiser makes it even less appealing to play. Folding is the standard and mathematically correct play here.

I am in the big blind with Ace-high, specifically Ah-6c. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and Pro folded. With 200 more to call into a pot of 450, the pot odds are quite favorable at 2.25:1. While playing out of position post-flop is a disadvantage, Ace-high is strong enough to defend, especially against a button opening range which is typically wide. Calling is the most disciplined and standard play here.

The board is T-T-T, which is an extremely dry and static texture. My Ace-high is currently likely the best hand unless my opponent has a Ten (for quads) or we are chopping with another Ace. Betting into this board serves little purpose as it is unlikely to get value from worse hands while inviting raises that I cannot call. Checking is the optimal play to maintain pot control and see how the opponent proceeds.

The flop came out triple Tens. My hand is King-high, but on this specific board, any pocket pair forms a full house (e.g., 2-2-T-T-T) which beats my three of a kind with a King kicker. Additionally, any Ace-high hand currently has me out-kicked. Since Flashlight checked, checking back is the most sensible play to control the pot size and see a free turn card. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I likely won't fold out a pocket pair or an Ace, and I'm ahead of most non-Ace air that would fold anyway.

The board shows three Tens, creating a situation where any Ten gives the opponent quad Tens. Additionally, a nine or any pocket pair gives the opponent a full house. My Ace-high has showdown value against pure air, but there is no value in betting here as I will only be called by better hands. Since the opponent checked the flop, they likely also have a weak range or a marginal holding. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity if the opponent is also bluffing with air.

The turn card is a 9, which doesn't improve my King-high but could have given Flashlight a full house if they are holding a 9. Given that Flashlight checked again, I will check back to see a free river card. King-high still has some showdown value, and I'd like to reach the showdown as cheaply as possible or improve on the river.

The board is T-T-T-9-K. My Ace-high is a marginal hand that serves only as a bluff-catcher. Since my opponent has checked behind on the flop, turn, and river, it is highly likely that neither of us has a strong hand or that my opponent is inducing a bluff. Betting would not generate value as my opponent will fold their air and call with any King or Nine, which beats me. Checking is the optimal play to reach showdown and realize any remaining equity.

On a board of 10-10-10-9-K, I have the second-best possible hand (Tens full of Kings) using the King that hit on the river. The only hand that beats me is a four-of-a-kind (a Ten in the hole), which is highly unlikely given Flashlight's passive checking on all streets. I need to bet for value here to get paid by any hands like a 9 (Tens full of Nines), pocket pairs, or even Ace-high. A bet of around 60% of the pot should be small enough to be called by marginal hands but large enough to extract decent value.

The board texture of T-T-T-9-K is extremely dry, and the opponent's check-check-check line on the previous streets strongly suggests they do not have a Ten or a strong full house. My Ace-high hand (A-10-10-10-K) functions as a strong bluff-catcher here, as it beats any missed draw or weaker pair the opponent might be betting for thin value or as a bluff (such as a 9, 8, or lower). Given the pot odds of 3.5:1 (calling 400 into a 1400 pot), I only need to be right roughly 22% of the time for this to be profitable. Betting or raising would likely fold out the bluffs I beat and only get called by hands that have me crushed. Therefore, calling is the optimal play to induce and catch a bluff.

With Ad3d on the Button in a 3-handed game, we have a strong starting hand that plays well post-flop and can hit nut flushes. Raising to 250 is a standard open to try and steal the blinds or take the betting lead with position.

I am in the Small Blind with a weak hand (Ks 4c) and I am out of position against an opponent (Pro) who has raised on the button. K4 offsuit has poor playability post-flop and provides no equity incentive to call the raise. Folding is the most disciplined decision to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 7c, which is a strong suited one-gapper that plays very well post-flop. Pro raised from the Button to 250, and Flashlight folded. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 400, giving me pot odds of approximately 2.6:1. Given the strength of my hand and the favorable price, I will call to see a flop and defend my blind.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needs a 10 for a straight) on a board of Kd-8s-Jh. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser (Pro) who is on the button. I am first to act and will check to see how the aggressor plays this flop. My 9-high hand has very little showdown value, but I have some equity if a 10 comes.

The flop Kd 8s Jh connects well with the Big Blind's calling range, which contains a lot of broadways, Kx, and Jx hands. Our Ad3d only has an overcard and a backdoor flush draw. Checking behind allows us to control the pot size and possibly hit a diamond or Ace on the turn, while avoiding getting check-raised or floated on a board that doesn't heavily favor our range.

The turn 3h is a blank for my hand, but it adds a heart flush draw to the board. After Pro checked back on the flop (Kd-8s-Jh), which is a board texture that typically favors the pre-flop raiser, their range is somewhat capped. However, I only have 9-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10). Checking is the best play here to see if I can get a free card to the river or see how Pro reacts. If Pro bets, I'll likely fold given my low equity and the fact that I'm out of position.

With the opponent checking again on the turn, our pair of threes with an Ace kicker has marginal showdown value. By checking behind, we keep the pot controlled, realize our equity by seeing a free river, and avoid getting check-raised by any disguised strong hands or flush draws.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-high on a board of Kd-8s-Jh-3h-6s. The action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn, which suggests that the pre-flop raiser (Pro) likely has a marginal hand or a weak holding they are content to take to showdown. While I have zero showdown value with 9-high, attempting to bluff here is risky because the board contains several cards that hit a typical button-opening range (K, J, 8). If I bet, I am unlikely to fold out a Jack or even a medium-strength 8, and my check on the turn makes it difficult to represent a strong King. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or simply fold if Pro decides to bet.

On the river, Flash checks for the third time. We have a pair of 3s with an Ace kicker, which has decent showdown value against missed draws like QT, T9, AQ, or AT. Betting here accomplishes little, as worse hands will fold and better hands (like an 8, 6, J, or pocket pair) will call. Checking behind allows us to safely get to showdown and realize our hand's showdown value.

I am on the button with a weak hand, Queen-Three offsuit. This hand has very little equity and poor playability in a 3-handed game. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack early in the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with K7o. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro). K7o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-vs-blind situation to put pressure on the opponent and potentially win the pot immediately. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

Flash is opening frequently and, coming from the Small Blind, his range is likely quite wide. A7o is a decent hand heads-up and being in position gives us a significant advantage post-flop. Calling the extra 200 chips to defend the big blind and evaluate the flop is a standard and solid play.

I have flopped top and bottom two pair (Kings and Sevens) on a board of K-T-7. This is a very strong hand. I will lead out with a continuation bet to get value from weaker hands like K-x, T-x, and straight draws such as QJ or J9. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard sizing that allows me to build the pot while keeping Pro's range wide.

On a board of Ts Kh 7d, our bottom pair with an Ace kicker is weak against a c-bet from the pre-flop raiser. The opponent's range hits this board quite well (Kings, Tens, broadway combinations), and we have no strong redraws. With only 5 outs to improve to two pair or trips, folding is the safest and most optimal play to preserve our stack for better spots.

I am on the button with QJo, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. I have been consistently opening for 300 chips in previous hands when I'm the first to enter the pot. Maintaining this sizing keeps my range balanced and puts pressure on the blinds. QJo has good playability post-flop, and opening here is standard professional strategy.

I am dealt 2c 4s offsuit in the Small Blind, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Flash has raised from the Button. Playing this hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser is a losing proposition, so the easiest and most standard play is to simply fold.

The hand 3d 2s is one of the weakest starting hands in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Even with the pot odds provided, playing out of position against a button raise with such a weak hand is mathematically negative expected value. Folding is the correct, disciplined decision to preserve the stack.

72 suited is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. From the Button, despite having position post-flop, playing a hand this poor is unadvisable as it easily leads to dominated situations and losing chips. The optimal move is to fold.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (5h 2c). Completing the blind puts me out of position against the Big Blind with a hand that has very low equity and poor playability post-flop. Folding is the disciplined, standard, and profitable long-term choice here to preserve my stack.

8-2 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. It has poor board coverage and almost no equity against any reasonable range. In a tournament setting, playing speculative trash hands from the button is inefficient and chip-suicidal, so the best move is to fold.

I am in the small blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. While it only costs 50 more to call and see a flop, this hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position against the Big Blind for the remainder of the hand. To maintain my chip lead and avoid unnecessary risks with junk hands, I will fold.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Even in a three-handed game, 94o does not have enough equity to justify a raise or a limp, especially with active players in the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With 7d Td, a suited one-gapper, and it folded to me in the Small Blind, this is a strong hand to attack the Big Blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Flashlight to fold weak hands and plays reasonably well post-flop if called.

I am in the big blind with a weak hand, Queen-3 offsuit. Calling a 3x raise out of position with such a disconnected and low-equity hand is strategically unsound. It is better to fold and wait for a more favorable spot to preserve my stack.
Holding 94o on the Button is an extremely weak, unplayable starting hand. There is no reason to put chips into the pot, so the correct and standard play is to fold and wait for better opportunities.

With suited connectors 8c 7c in the small blind and the button having folded, completing the call is a standard and profitable play to see a flop heads-up against the big blind, leveraging the playability of my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 6h. The Button (Pro) has folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has just completed the blind. Since I have position for the rest of the hand and a mediocre starting hand, checking to see a free flop is the most sensible play to minimize risk and realize my equity.

The flop contains an Ace, which hits the Big Blind's range very effectively. Holding 8-high with minimal backdoor potential, I have no equity to bet for value or as a semi-bluff. Checking is the standard play to re-evaluate the turn or fold to any significant aggression.

I have 9-high on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. My opponent (Flashlight) checked to me. While I could attempt to bluff, there is very little equity in this hand if I am called, and checking back allows me to see a turn for free and potentially realize some thin equity or catch a bluff later if the board texture changes. Checking is the most prudent play here.

I am in the small blind holding a flush draw with 8c7c on a board of 5h Ac Jd Jc. Since the board is paired and I am out of position, checking is the optimal strategy to control the pot size and realize equity. I want to see the river for as cheaply as possible or potentially check-call if the opponent bets, provided the sizing is reasonable. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or hands with significant equity, putting me in a difficult spot.

I have 9-high with no draw on a board of A-J-5-J. Both Flashlight and I checked the flop, and Flashlight has checked again on the turn. There is very little value in betting here as I have no showdown value and little equity. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially win if the hand goes to a showdown with no further betting, or if I somehow improve, though that is unlikely. Maintaining a pot control strategy in this small, limped pot is the most sensible approach.

I have rivered an Ace-high flush (Ac-Jc-Tc-8c-7c) and I am first to act. Given the previous passive play (check-check-check), my opponent is unlikely to have a full house. I should bet to extract value from their weaker holdings like top pair (Aces) or two-pair hands that might call a reasonable bet. A sizing of 125 into a 200 pot is balanced to encourage a call while maximizing value.

I have 9-high with absolutely no showdown value on a board that contains an Ace, two Jacks, and a Ten. Flashlight has bet 125 into a 200 pot after checking the flop and turn. While it's possible Flashlight is bluffing, my hand cannot even beat most of Flashlight's own potential bluffs (like King-high or Queen-high). Raising as a bluff is unnecessary in such a small pot during the early stages of the tournament. The most disciplined and risk-averse play is to fold.

I am on the button with 8d-7h, which is a marginal hand with low equity in a three-handed game. Acting first pre-flop makes it difficult to play this hand profitably without a strong range. Given the tournament context and the strength of the cards, folding is the most disciplined and stack-efficient strategy.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. Ace-four offsuit is a strong enough hand to raise for value and fold equity against the Big Blind in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately.
I am in the Big Blind with 7d 4h, a very weak, unsuited, and unconnected hand. The Small Blind raised to 300 (3x the big blind). With a trash hand out of position, the only sensible play is to fold.

I am on the button with King-Eight suited (Kh 8h), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Opening from the button allows me to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play a strong hand in position post-flop. A standard 3x open to 300 chips is appropriate here to build the pot and discourage light calls from the blinds.

Facing a 3bb raise from the Button, Queen-Jack offsuit is a marginal holding from the Small Blind. Playing out of position against an aggressive opener with a hand that has high reverse implied odds (often dominated by KQ, AQ, AJ) makes calling unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am in the big blind with 7c 4c against an aggressive opener. While the pot odds are tempting, 7-4 suited is a bottom-tier hand and does not perform well enough against a button raising range to justify calling out of position. Folding is the disciplined play to avoid committing chips with a dominated hand.
7c 6s is an offsuit connector, which is too weak to open-raise or call with from the Button in a 3-handed game. Folding is the optimal play to preserve my stack for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit after the Button folded. Since the Big Blind is the only opponent left, the pot offers decent odds to complete the small blind and see a flop. While J8o is not a premium hand, it is playable in a blind-vs-blind situation. I will complete the bet to see a flop heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen suited, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has simply completed the blind, suggesting a relatively weak range. I will raise for value and to take control of the pot. A raise to 400 is a standard size to build the pot while having position for the rest of the hand.

The Big Blind raised to 400 after I completed from the Small Blind. Holding Jack-Eight offsuit, I am significantly out of position and the hand is too weak to continue against an aggressive raise. It is better to preserve my stack for a more profitable situation than to invest further chips in a marginal hand.

8-9 offsuit is a weak, marginal hand that does not play well in a multi-way pot. Despite being on the button, the risk-reward ratio of calling a 100-chip bet to see a flop is unfavorable. It is more profitable to play conservatively and wait for a stronger starting hand to maximize stack preservation.

Holding 26o in the small blind is one of the weakest starting hands possible. Although it only costs 50 more to call and see a flop, this hand performs poorly post-flop and puts me in a difficult position against the big blind. I'll choose to fold and preserve my chip lead rather than play a marginal hand out of position.

I am on the button with K3o in a three-handed game. My stack is the largest at the table. Given the tournament dynamics so far, the blinds have been folding frequently to 3x opens. While K3o is a marginal hand, it is strong enough to open from the button to apply pressure on Pro and Flashlight. I will continue my aggressive opening strategy with a standard 3x raise.

K6o is a very weak, disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the Button while in the Small Blind means we would be playing out of position with a hand that has huge reverse implied odds and is often dominated. Folding is the only sensible play.

Ace-Five suited is a strong hand for defending the big blind against a button open. With favorable pot odds and good post-flop equity, calling is the standard play to see a flop. Raising (3-betting) is an option, but with an aggressive opponent, keeping the pot manageable while playing in position against the button is a solid strategic choice.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high (Ac 5c) on a board of Qd 8d Qs. The board is paired, and the pre-flop aggressor (the Button) has a range that significantly overlaps with this board (many Queens and overpairs). My hand is weak and has low equity against a continuing range. I should check to the aggressor to evaluate their action and keep the pot small.

As the pre-flop aggressor, the Qd-8d-Qs board is a favorable texture for a continuation bet. Paired boards are difficult for the big blind defender to hit significantly, and King-high still holds decent showdown value if checked down, but I prefer to take the pot now. A bet of roughly 46% of the pot (300 chips) is sufficient to fold out many of the opponent's misses while keeping my bluffs efficient.

The board is paired with two Queens, which strongly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. My Ace-high hand has minimal showdown value and very little equity to improve against a continuing range. Calling a bet here is a losing play as I am likely behind and have no path to a winning hand unless I improve drastically. Folding is the optimal decision to preserve my stack.
Holding offsuit low connectors (4d 5h) on the Button in a 3-handed game is an easy fold pre-flop. The hand has very little equity and poor playability.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand (3d 2s). Given that the potential return does not justify the risk of playing such a poor hand out of position against the big blind, the most disciplined play is to fold and conserve my stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with 7s 6s, a strong suited connector that plays well in position. The pot is currently just the blinds (150). With 8750 chips (87.5 big blinds), I have plenty of room to maneuver. Opening with a raise is the standard aggressive play to seize initiative and potentially steal the blinds or build a pot in position. I will raise to 300 to thin the field and gain control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind holding 4d 3h, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised from the Button to 300. Calling out of position with one of the worst starting hands in poker is not profitable, especially with the Big Blind still to act. Folding is the clear and standard play here.

ATo is a strong hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. Calling allows us to see a flop and keep the opponent's wider opening range in the hand, whereas a 3-bet might fold out hands we dominate.

We flopped top pair, top kicker, along with the nut flush draw since we hold the Ace of Spades on a monotone flop. This is a monster hand. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard line is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Checking allows Flashlight to continuation bet with their bluffs or worse made hands, and we can decide whether to call or check-raise based on their sizing.

I flopped a flush draw on a monochromatic board (Ts 4s 3s) while holding the 7s 6s. As the pre-flop aggressor in position, this is a clear continuation bet scenario. Betting allows me to take down the pot immediately if my opponent has missed, or build the pot for when I hit my flush. Given the pot is 650, a bet of 350 is appropriate to apply pressure without overcommitting if I'm raised.

We flopped top pair with top kicker, and we hold the Ace of Spades, giving us the nut flush draw on this monotone board. Against a Button c-bet, we have a very strong hand. While a check-raise could build the pot and get value from worse flush draws or worse top pairs, it might also force our opponent to fold all their bluffs and weaker made hands. By just calling, we keep Flashlight's entire range intact, including hands we heavily dominate, while controlling the pot out of position. We are perfectly happy to see a turn card, as any spade gives us the absolute nuts, and our top pair is often already the best hand.

The turn card is a blank 7h, changing very little about the board. I still have top pair, top kicker, and the nut flush draw. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most profitable line here. It allows Flashlight to continue firing with their bluffs, semi-bluffs, and worse value hands (like worse tens or underpairs) that they might otherwise fold if I were to donk-bet out of position.

I have flopped a flush (7s 6s on a Ts 4s 3s board). The turn (7h) did not change the board texture significantly. Since the opponent checked to me, this is a clear value betting opportunity. A bet of 700 into a 1350 pot is approximately 50%, which is a solid size to extract value from hands like a set or a pair plus a spade draw, while maintaining control of the pot.

Facing a double barrel on the turn, my top pair, top kicker plus the nut flush draw remains a very strong hand. Calling is the most profitable option because it keeps the opponent's bluffs in the pot and controls the size of the pot in case they already have a made flush or a set. By calling, I maintain disguised hand strength and can evaluate the river card, giving myself a chance to hit the nut flush or see a showdown with top pair.

On the river, my hand improves to top two pair (Aces and Tens) and I hold the Ace of Spades, which is a key blocker to the nut flush. Having check-called both the flop and turn, the standard and most profitable line is to check again to the aggressor. This allows Flashlight to fire a third barrel with missed draws as a bluff, or to value bet worse hands like a weaker Ace. Checking also keeps the pot controlled in the event they already have a made flush.

I have successfully made a flush with my 7s 6s on a board of Ts 4s 3s 7h Ac. Although my flush is relatively low, it is a very strong hand on this board. My opponent checked to me on the river. Given the pot size of 2750, I should look to extract value from hands like sets, two pairs, or weaker flushes that the opponent may have been calling with. A bet of 1000 (roughly 36% of the pot) is appropriate to induce calls from these second-best hands while managing the risk of facing a check-raise from a potential higher flush.

I have top two pair (Aces and Tens) and hold the Ace of Spades, blocking the nut flush. Facing a relatively small river bet of 1000 into a pot of 2750, my hand is far too strong to fold. However, raising would likely only be called by better hands, such as flushes or sets, while folding out the bluffs and weaker value bets. Calling is the optimal decision to get to showdown against hands like AK, AQ, or missed flush draws.

I am on the button with 7c 3c. In a three-handed game, this hand is significantly below the threshold for a standard opening range. While suited, the gap between the 7 and 3 makes it difficult to play post-flop, and I have no need to take unnecessary risks with a weak hand early in the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With Q8o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a marginal hand. Playing out of position against the Big Blind with a weak, disconnected offsuit hand often leads to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is a disciplined choice to conserve chips and wait for a better spot to attack or defend.
Q6o is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button, it is too weak to open raise with, especially considering I have a slightly shorter stack and want to preserve chips for better spots.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand, 4-10 offsuit. Since the button folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Calling would require putting in another 50 chips, but this hand has very poor playability post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined and standard action in this spot.
Holding 9c 3h, which is a weak offsuit hand, I should fold as there is no incentive to enter the pot from the button position against two opponents.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). While I could complete the bet for 50 more or raise to steal the blinds, T7o is a weak hand to play out of position against Pro, who has shown a tendency to be sticky in previous hands (like Hand #19). I have J-2 offsuit, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the button, I have very little equity and poor playability out of position. Folding is the only reasonable choice here.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Seven suited, which is a strong hand for defending against a button raise. I am out of position as the big blind against the pre-flop aggressor on a Queen-Ten-Three board. Holding Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw, I have some equity but not enough to lead into the raiser. Checking is the optimal standard play to control the pot size and see how my opponent responds.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Nine offsuit from the button and Flashlight called from the big blind. The flop comes Queen-Ten-Three with two clubs. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should continuation bet frequently here to try and take the pot down immediately. A bet of roughly half the pot is standard to apply pressure to hands like mid-pairs or weak draws that might fold.

The board of Qc-Th-3c is relatively connected, but with an Ace-high hand and a backdoor flush draw, I have enough equity to continue against a standard continuation bet. The pot offers over 3:1 odds, making a call a profitable decision to see the turn card, especially given that Flash likely has a wide range on the button. Folding here would be overly conservative, and raising would be ill-advised given I am out of position.

I have improved to a pair of sevens on the turn. As the out-of-position player against a pre-flop aggressor who continuation-bet the flop, I should continue to play cautiously. My hand is a medium-strength bluff catcher, and checking allows me to control the pot size and see how Flash proceeds. If Flash checks behind, I can evaluate the river cheaply; if Flash bets, I can decide whether to call based on the sizing and his perceived range.

I have Ace-high and the turn 7s doesn't significantly change the board texture. Flashlight called my flop continuation bet, which suggests they have at least a piece of the board (like a Ten or a Queen) or a draw. Since I don't have much fold equity against a range that called the flop and I want to keep the pot small to reach a cheap showdown or potentially hit an Ace on the river, checking behind is the most prudent play.

The board has brought a pair of sevens with a potential straight completing on the river (J-9). With only a pair of sevens, my hand is strictly a bluff-catcher. Since Flash checked back the turn, it is unlikely they are holding a very strong hand, but they may still have a queen or a pair that beats me. Betting would likely only be called by better hands, and checking allows me to potentially win at showdown against missed draws or weaker holdings. Checking is the most balanced and conservative play here.

I have Ace-high (As9c) on a board of Qc-Th-3c-7s-8s. Flashlight called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, but has checked the turn and river. While my Ace-high has some showdown value against missed club draws or hands like KJ, it loses to any pair (like a Ten or a Seven). Flashlight's line of check-calling the flop and then checking the turn and river suggests either a weak made hand or a missed draw. Given the board texture and the action, I don't expect a bet to fold out a better hand (like a Ten) often enough to make it profitable, and I beat their pure bluffs. I will check behind to take the free showdown.

On the Button in a 3-handed game, 9c6c is a borderline hand. It has some playability being suited, but with a 2-gap and relatively low ranks, it's often dominated and can lead to tricky spots post-flop if called. To preserve chips and avoid marginal situations, folding is the disciplined choice here.

In the small blind, with the button having folded, I have a clear opportunity to take the lead against the big blind. JTo is a solid holding in this heads-up scenario. A raise to 300 is a standard move to build the pot and exert pressure, potentially winning the blinds immediately or playing the hand with the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 8c. The Button (Pro) has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. I have position on Flashlight for the rest of the hand. 98 offsuit is a decent hand to defend with in a blind-versus-blind situation, as it has good connectivity and realizes its equity well when in position. The pot odds are 2:1 (I need to call 200 to win a total pot of 600), making this a standard call to see a flop.

I am out of position in the small blind against the big blind. The flop (7c 6s Qc) is relatively dry, and my holding (Jh Tc) has minimal equity, only a backdoor flush draw and an overcard. A C-bet here is unlikely to force a fold from a range that includes many queens, middle pairs, or strong high cards that would have defended the big blind. Therefore, checking to pot-control and re-evaluate on the turn is the most prudent strategy.

On this flop of 7c 6s Qc, I have an open-ended straight draw (9-8 on a 7-6 board). Flashlight, who raised pre-flop, has checked to me. While I could bet as a semi-bluff to try and take the pot down now, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit my straight on the turn. Since I have position, I can see how Flashlight reacts to the turn card before committing more chips.

I have improved to a pair of Jacks on the turn, which is a strong holding given the previous passive action (checks on the flop). I want to extract value from any draws (like 8-9 or flush draws) or weaker hands like middle pairs or low-kicker Queens. A bet here also helps to define the opponent's range before the river. I will bet 350 into the 600 pot to maintain control and build the pot.

On a board of 7c-6s-Qc-Jd, I have an open-ended straight draw with 9s-8c. Flashlight checked the flop but is now leading for 350 into a 600-chip pot (total pot 950). I need to call 350 to see the river. My pot odds are approximately 27% (350/1300). With 8 outs to a straight (four 5s and four 10s), my probability of hitting on the river is about 17.4%. While I don't have the immediate pot odds to call, I am in position and have significant implied odds if I hit my straight, particularly with a 5 which would be quite deceptive. Flashlight's lead on the turn suggests they may have improved to a pair of Jacks or are continuing with a Queen, meaning I can likely extract more value on the river if I hit. Therefore, a call is the most profitable play here.

I am out of position against the Big Blind with a pair of Jacks. The river card (3h) is a blank and does not complete any obvious draws. My pair of Jacks is a medium-strength hand that is likely ahead of bluffs or lower pairs but loses to any Queen or better. Betting here is risky as it might isolate me against better hands that would call, while checking allows me to get to showdown, potentially inducing a bluff from the opponent or checking down to realize my equity.

I have 9-high after missing my open-ended straight draw on the river (9-8 on a 7-6-Q-J-3 board). Flashlight raised pre-flop, checked the flop, bet the turn, and has now checked the river. This line often indicates a marginal made hand like a weak Queen or a Jack that is looking to get to a cheap showdown, or a missed draw. While I could attempt a bluff to win the 1300-chip pot, Flashlight has shown a tendency to be somewhat 'sticky' in previous hands (e.g., Hand #5, Hand #32). Given my chip lead and the risk of being called by a marginal pair, checking back is the most prudent play to conserve my stack, as 9-high has almost no showdown value but the probability of a successful bluff against this opponent is not high enough to justify the bet.

I am on the button with suited connectors (6d 5d), which is a strong hand for playing in position. Given that no action has occurred yet and I am looking to steal the blinds, raising is the standard play here to put pressure on the opponents and potentially win the pot pre-flop or see a flop with initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with 9h 7h, which is a decent speculative hand. Flashlight opened from the Button for 3x the big blind. Calling 250 to potentially win a pot of 600+ gives me good pot odds, and the hand has strong post-flop playability. While I will be out of position, the stack depths allow for a call to see a flop. I prefer calling over raising here to keep the pot controlled and avoid a potential 4-bet from Flashlight or a squeeze from Pro in the Big Blind.

AKo is a premium hand that plays exceptionally well as a squeeze from the Big Blind, especially after a Button open and a Small Blind flat call. A large 3-bet will often win the pot pre-flop or isolate a single opponent, allowing us to take the betting lead. I'll size up to 1300 to maximize fold equity and value.

I am in position on the button with suited connectors (6d 5d) after opening to 300. The Big Blind has squeezed to 1300. While 6d 5d is a speculative hand, the price to call (1000 more) is reasonable relative to the pot size, and being in position gives me a significant advantage to outplay the opponent if I connect with the flop. Pro has executed a significant squeeze from the Big Blind, and Flashlight's call suggests a strong range or at least a pair that wants to see a flop. As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I want to maintain the betting lead. The Q-T-6 rainbow board gives me a gutshot straight draw to the nuts and two overcards, providing decent equity even when called. The flop of Q-T-6 is not favorable for my bottom pair of 6s, especially against an opponent who 3-bet pre-flop and is now betting into me. I am on the Button with Qh 9c, which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. I will make a typical raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot in position.

KTo is a strong enough hand to open-raise from the button in a 3-handed game. I will make a standard raise to 300 to steal the blinds or play the pot in position.

The flop of Kd-Tc-Th is heavily weighted toward the button's opening range, hitting many Kings, Tens, and potentially pairs that beat my Jack-high. With Ten-Seven offsuit in the small blind, my hand is relatively weak and playing out of position against the big blind is disadvantageous. Given the current stack size and tournament stage, folding is the most disciplined and conservative play to preserve my chip stack.

On the turn card 9d, the board texture remains relatively dry, though it introduces some marginal straight draws (J-8, 8-7). Having flopped trips with a King kicker, I am in a very strong position. I am in the position of checking on the river. The board is paired (Tens) and there are multiple straight and flush possibilities available (7-6 straight, diamond flush). I am on the button with 8h 3c, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. While I am in a good position to steal, both Pro and Flashlight have been active and willing to call or re-raise. With 8d7d in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop. I am dealt a very weak offsuit hand (Q2o) and am first to act on the Button. In a 3-handed game, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify opening, so the best decision is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind facing the Big Blind. Queen-Deuce suited is a weak hand, but in a blind-versus-blind situation, it is often profitable to complete and see a flop for a small investment to maintain position and control over the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. Flashlight completed from the Small Blind. Ten-Nine is a strong enough hand to consider a raise, but checking allows me to see a flop for free and take advantage of my position on all subsequent betting rounds. I am in the Small Blind with Queen-high on an Ace-high board. Since the Big Blind has a range that heavily includes Aces, betting here would be a low-success-rate bluff. Checking is the standard play to realize my equity for free or potentially check-fold to aggression if the opponent bets.

On this Ace-high board, I have no direct connection or draw with my Ten-high. Flashlight, who completed from the Small Blind, has checked to me. I am in the button position with a very weak hand (Queen-deuce offsuit). Given my stack size and the strength of the hand, there is no strategic value in opening the pot or calling. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard opening hand. Raising to 3x the big blind is consistent with my previous patterns and allows me to take control of the hand with position on both the Small Blind and the Big Blind.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a three-handed game. Flashlight has raised from the Small Blind, and I have position on them for the rest of the hand. I am on the button with 4c 7d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. There is no strategic value in playing this hand against two opponents. Folding is the clear, disciplined choice to conserve my stack for more profitable situations.

Q9o is a weak, easily dominated hand. Although we are in position against the Small Blind, defending against a 3x open with this hand will frequently lead to difficult post-flop situations where we are second best. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 3s 2c, which is the worst possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, folding is the only viable option.

AcQc is a premium starting hand, and I am in the best position (Button). Raising to 300 is a standard open to build the pot and thin the field, capitalizing on both my strong equity and positional advantage.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (3h 8h) facing a raise from the button (Pro). Calling out of position against an aggressive button raiser with a hand that has poor connectivity and low high-card value is not profitable. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

On a flop of Th-Qs-Kd, I have an open-ended straight draw (any Nine or Ace makes a straight). The opponent (Pro) has bet 325 into a pot of 650, giving me 3-to-1 pot odds. The turn brings the Queen of Hearts, pairing the board. I still hold an open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or a Nine), but this board is very coordinated and likely hits my opponent's range. The turn card improves my hand to trip Queens with an Ace kicker. On the turn, the board has paired with the Queen of Hearts (Th-Qs-Kd-Qh), and I still hold an open-ended straight draw with my Jack-Five (needing an Ace or a Nine). Betting here as a bluff is ineffective because my opponent will rarely fold any piece of this board (like a pair) and will certainly call or raise with a straight. I have some showdown value, so checking behind to realize it is the most logical and safest play.

The river is the Ten of Hearts, completing a dry board. My pair of nines is a marginal holding with some showdown value. Given that both players checked the flop and turn, the likelihood that my opponent has a significantly better hand is uncertain, but betting risks being called only by hands that have me beaten or folding out hands I might already be ahead of. I have a pair of Sevens with an Eight kicker. The action has been extremely passive, with Flashlight limping pre-flop and checking every street. I am on the button with a decent, albeit not premium, hand (9s Tc). The flop comes 9h 5h Qd, and I hold 4c 6s. My hand has completely missed this board, offering no pair or significant draw. Given the lack of equity and being out of position against the big blind, checking is the most prudent action to minimize potential losses.

I have 7-high on a board of Qh 9h 5d. I have no pair and no real draws. Flashlight checked to me in a limped pot. There is no reason to bluff into a small pot with zero equity. Checking back to see a free turn card is the most prudent play here.

I am in the small blind with 4c 6s on a board of 9h 5h Qd Kc. My hand has completely missed the board, and I have no pair or viable draws. Given that I am out of position and have zero equity, checking is the only logical action to take to minimize my losses and see the river without further investment.

I have 7-high with no draws on a board containing two overcards to my hand. In a small limped pot where Flashlight has checked for the second time, there is no reason to attempt a bluff or build the pot with zero equity. I will check behind to see the river for free.

I am in the small blind holding 4c 6s. The board is 9h 5h Qd Kc Ts. I have completely missed this board, holding only high card six-high. With no equity and the opponent having checked, there is no value in betting as I cannot win a showdown. Checking allows me to reach showdown for free, though I expect to lose. Betting would be a pointless bluff into a player who checked behind on every street, and it is highly unlikely to generate a fold.

I have 7-high on a board where many common cards (Q, K, T, 9) or any Jack for a straight would beat me. Flashlight has checked every street in this limped pot, indicating a weak hand, but even a small pair or a better high card wins. Given the tiny pot size of 200 chips, there is little incentive to bluff, as the risk outweighs the reward. I will check back and see if my 7-high somehow wins or, more likely, concede the small pot.

I am holding King-Four offsuit on the button. This is a weak hand and not worth committing chips with, especially given the stack sizes and the potential for aggressive play from the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Q2o. Flashlight (Button) has folded, so the action is on me. The cost to call is only 50 to see a flop against Pro (Big Blind), who has a larger stack than me. While Q2o is a weak hand, the pot odds for calling are decent. However, in a three-handed game approaching the end of the tournament (hand 130/150), I prefer a 'fold or raise' strategy from the Small Blind to put pressure on the Big Blind or simply avoid playing weak hands out of position. Given that Q2o is extremely weak and Pro has been active, I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 11k stack.

I am on the button with Deuce of Diamonds and Four of Spades, which is a very weak starting hand. The pot is small (150) and while I have a large stack, there is no strategic reason to enter a pot with such a poor hand from any position, even on the button. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind with 9To. While it's a marginal offsuit hand, it has decent connectivity. As the chip leader, it's a good spot to apply pressure on the short-stacked Big Blind and attempt to steal the dead money in the pot. I'll make a standard raise; if I face a 3-bet, I can easily fold without much loss.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak, unsuited hand (6c 4h). While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), this hand has very poor playability and is dominated by almost any range that raises from the Small Blind. Given my relatively short stack compared to the table leaders, I need to preserve my chips and wait for a more favorable spot to engage, rather than calling with trash out of position.

Q7o is an offsuit, disconnected hand that plays poorly post-flop. Even from the button, it is not strong enough to open-raise, especially with a comfortable chip lead. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. With suited cards Jh5h, completing the small blind is a standard play to see a flop cheaply against the big blind, as the pot odds are favorable for a call. I will complete the bet to 100.

I am in the big blind with T8 suited. The action folded to Flashlight in the small blind who just completed the blind. Since I am already in the hand for the big blind and there is no raise to call, I have the option to check and see a flop for free or raise to try and take the pot immediately. Given my stack size and the strength of a suited connector like T8, checking is a standard and low-risk play to realize my equity in a small pot.

I hold Jack-high on a King-high board. I have no pair and no strong draw, so betting here would be a pure bluff without much fold equity. Checking is the standard play to re-evaluate the next card or to check-fold if the opponent bets.

On a flop of 7h-Kd-8s, I have second pair with my Tens of clubs and Eight of clubs. Flashlight completed from the small blind and checked the flop, which often indicates a wide, potentially weak range. While my pair of eights has value, the board contains an overcard (King). Checking back is a solid play here to control the pot size and see a turn card for free, maintaining the opportunity to reach a cheap showdown or evaluate based on the turn action.

I am in the small blind against the big blind with a flush draw. The board is dry, and I have minimal showdown value with Jack-high. Checking allows me to see the river for free if the opponent also checks, which is the most prudent strategy for a short stack without a made hand.

On the turn, the board is 7h-Kd-8s-3d. Flashlight has checked for the second time after completing the small blind. My second pair (Eights) remains a strong hand in this blind-versus-blind scenario, especially since the 3d is a blank. By checking back again, I am exercising pot control and ensuring I reach the river with a hand that has significant showdown value without risking a check-raise from a slow-played King or a trapped monster. This keeps the pot small and manageable while allowing Flashlight to potentially bluff on the river or call a value bet later.

I am on the river with absolute air (Jack-high). The board is King-high with straight and pair possibilities. Since my opponent checked to me, there is no value in betting as a bluff because a pair or better will call, and I cannot improve. Checking is the only logical move to avoid unnecessary loss.

On the river, the board is 7h-Kd-8s-3d-9s. I hold middle pair (Eights). Flashlight has checked for the third consecutive street after completing the small blind pre-flop. This sequence of checks from Flashlight strongly suggests a weak range, likely consisting of a smaller pair, a missed draw, or complete air. While I might get called by a Seven or a Nine if I bet small, the pot is only 200 chips. Checking back is the safest way to realize my showdown value and win the pot without risking a check-raise in a situation where my hand is rarely beaten but also unlikely to get significant value from worse hands.

As the short stack on the button with suited connectors (6c 9c), I need to be aggressive to try and steal the blinds. Raising to 300 is a standard open to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300, which is their standard opening size. My hand is definitely strong enough to continue against a button open, especially with Flashlight being the short stack at around 39 BBs. While calling is an option to keep the pot smaller, a 3-bet would likely fold out their weaker steals or force a decision for their tournament life. However, given the tournament context and my position, calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without overcommitting if the Big Blind (Pro) decides to squeeze. I will call the 250 more.

I am dealt Nine-Deuce offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, calling here out of position would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the only sensible option.

I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw on a very high-card-heavy board (J-K-T). This board likely connects with the button's opening range. Being out of position, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. Betting into them here doesn't accomplish much as they are unlikely to fold any piece of this board, and I have some showdown value and draw equity that I'd like to realize cheaply.

I have completely missed the flop with 6c 9c on a board of Jd Kh Tc, which heavily favors a calling range. With no pair and no meaningful draw, betting into this board is inefficient as I have no equity to improve against a calling range. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn for free, preserving my stack.

On a board of J-K-T-8, I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (any Queen). Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, checked back on the flop, which suggests they might not have a strong King or a made straight yet, or they are playing cautiously on a very coordinated board. The 8 on the turn is a blank for my specific hand but completes potential straights for any 9 in their range. Given I am out of position and have some showdown value plus equity to the nuts, checking is the most prudent play to see how Flashlight reacts to the turn card.

The board is Jd-Kh-Tc-8h. With 6c-9c, I have a gutshot straight draw needing a 7. The pot is 700 and I am in position on the button. Since I have already checked the turn, if the river is checked to me, I will check behind to keep the pot small and reach showdown, as my hand has no value to bet and no equity to improve beyond the gutshot. Checking is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and minimize losses.

On a highly coordinated board of J-K-T-8-7, my pair of sevens is extremely weak. There are numerous straights possible (any 9 or Q makes a straight). However, Flashlight (the button) has checked back both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a weak-to-medium hand or a surrender. By checking the river, I am looking to realize my showdown value cheaply. Betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me (any 8, 10, J, K, or straight), and checking allows me to potentially catch a bluff or win against a lower Ace-high if it goes to showdown.

Upon reflection, I have made a straight (7-8-9-T-J) with my 6c 9c on the board of Jd-Kh-Tc-8h-7h. Although I hold a straight, I am losing to any hand containing a Queen (specifically 9-Q or A-Q) which would form a higher straight. Given the small pot size of 700 and the possibility of being out-kicked by a higher straight, checking behind is a prudent way to capture the showdown value of my straight without risking being raised or value-betting into a better hand. I will stick to the plan of checking behind.

On the button with Ten-Nine offsuit in a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. Flashlight is relatively short-stacked, making them more likely to either fold or commit their stack, while Pro is the chip leader and has been playing relatively conservatively. A standard raise to 300 allows me to take control of the pot or win it pre-flop.
Facing a 3x open from the button, 6d4d is too weak of a hand to play from the small blind. Being out of position and not closing the pre-flop action makes this a clear fold.
Flash raised to 300 from the button. I am in the big blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The pot odds are favorable to call (200 to win a total of 450). I will call to see the flop.

The board is extremely dangerous with Ace-King-Queen. I hold a pair of Queens with a ten, which gives me a gutshot straight draw to a Jack. Given I am out of position and the board hits the pre-flop aggressor's range very hard, checking is the standard and safest play to evaluate the opponent's action.

The flop of Ace-King-Queen is very heavy with high cards, making it likely that the opponent has connected in some way. I have an inside straight draw to a Jack. Since I am in position and the opponent checked, I will check behind to take a free card and attempt to realize my equity without risking a check-raise on a board that likely hit the Big Blind's defending range.

The board (A-K-Q-5) is extremely coordinated and dangerous. I hold a pair of Queens, which is a marginal hand on this board. My opponent, Flash, has checked behind on the flop and turn, indicating either weakness or a trap. Betting is unnecessary as I am unlikely to get called by worse, and checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully reach a cheap showdown.

The turn 5s is a total brick and doesn't change the board state. I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (any Jack), but my hand currently has zero showdown value. By checking back, I ensure I see the river for free to realize my equity. The board is A-K-Q-5-3 with three clubs. My pair of Queens is very weak as it is outkicked by any Ace or King and loses to any Jack (straight). My opponent has checked both the flop and turn, suggesting they have a marginal hand or are also checking for showdown. Since I cannot get called by a worse hand and a bet might be raised by a better one, checking is the safest and most strategic play.

The board is extremely coordinated with Ace-King-Queen, and since the flop and turn were checked through, it is highly likely that Flashlight has at least some showdown value or a marginal piece of the board. My Ten-high has zero showdown value, but the board is so heavy with high cards that it likely hit the Big Blind's defending range. Betting now after checking twice would look suspicious and like a polarized bluff. Given the small pot size and the fact that I am in a comfortable chip position with only 16 hands remaining, I will check and concede the pot rather than risking chips on a low-frequency bluff.

A9o is a strong starting hand on the Button, easily in the top quartile of hands. I want to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position with the initiative. A standard 3x open raise to 300 chips accomplishes this perfectly.

I am in the Small Blind with KTo, a hand that has reasonable strength but plays poorly out of position. Pro has raised from the Button, suggesting a wide range. Calling allows me to see a flop with a decent holding while keeping the pot manageable. Since I already have 50 chips in the pot as the Small Blind, I need to call 250 more to match the raise.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Six diamonds (K6d). The button (Pro) opened for 300, and the small blind (Flashlight) called. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 900. K6d is a reasonable hand to defend in a multi-way pot, especially given the pot odds (getting 3.5 to 1). My stack is healthy at over 100 big blinds, and while K6 offsuit would be a marginal defend, the suitedness and high card value of the King make this a mandatory call in a three-way dynamic.

I am in the Small Blind with middle pair (Tens) on a coordinated board (8-T-7 with two spades). I am out of position against both the original raiser (Pro) and the Big Blind (Flash). With a vulnerable hand on a wet board, checking is the most strategic play to control the pot size and gauge the strength of my opponents before committing more chips. Betting would likely force out worse hands and isolate me against stronger holdings or semi-bluffs that have equity against me.

On this highly connected flop of 8s-Td-7s, I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (to the nine). On this highly coordinated flop (8s Td 7s), our Ac 9d gives us an open-ended straight draw along with an overcard. While a semi-bluff bet is an option, checking behind guarantees we see a free turn to realize our significant equity without risking a check-raise that might force us to fold a valuable draw.

The board remains relatively uncoordinated with the turn 2h, and the flop checked through, which suggests that neither the button nor the big blind has a powerhouse hand like a set. Checking is the most prudent play to navigate this pot cheaply out of position.

The board is 8s-Td-7s-2h. I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). After the flop went check-check-check, the turn 2h is a complete blank. Flashlight (SB) has checked to me. Given the tournament goal of maximizing stack and the fact that I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent play.

Both opponents have checked twice on a coordinated board, indicating marginal showdown value or missed draws. The turn 2h is a complete blank. With Ac 9d, I have an open-ended straight draw and an overcard, giving me strong equity if called. Betting here serves as an excellent semi-bluff, putting pressure on better Ace-highs and weak pairs to fold, allowing me to potentially pick up the 900-chip pot uncontested. If called, I still have many outs to improve to the best hand on the river.

Pro is betting 450 on the turn after the flop and turn were checked through by all players, which suggests a possible probe bet, a marginal made hand, or a missed draw. My hand (Tens) with a King kicker is likely the best hand, and a call allows me to evaluate the river while keeping the pot manageable. Raising could isolate me against a set or two-pair, while folding would be too weak given the passive nature of the action so far. Therefore, calling is the most strategic play to keep the pot size under control and see the final card.

On the turn 2h, the board remains highly connected (8s-Td-7s-2h). Pro has bet 450 after checking the flop, which could be a delayed continuation bet or a value bet with a marginal pair. However, the key factor is Flashlight calling the 450. In a three-way pot, a call from the Small Blind usually indicates a made hand (like a pair of Tens or Sevens) or a strong draw (like a flush draw or an open-ended straight draw). I am holding King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a nine). Mathematically, I need about 20% equity to call (450 to win a total pot of 2250), but my four outs to a straight only give me roughly 8.5% equity. Given that King-high has virtually no showdown value and I am facing both a bet and a call on a wet board, the most disciplined play is to fold.

I have flopped two pair (Kings and Tens) after the King landed on the river. The pot is currently 1800. My hand is very strong, and I should bet for value to get called by weaker hands like a Ten or an Eight. A bet of 600 represents a standard value sizing to get called by worse while minimizing risk if I am running into a trapped hand.

My hand is Ace-high as I completely missed my open-ended straight draw. The river brought the King of Spades, which completes the flush draw and connects with hands containing a King. Flashlight's decision to lead out for 600 after calling my turn semi-bluff strongly indicates they have a made hand, such as a flush or at least a pair of Kings. With zero showdown value and an unconvincing board for a bluff, folding is the only profitable play.
Pocket treys are a playable pocket pair in position on the button. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size to either steal the blinds or gain the initiative in the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with J9 of hearts, a decent suited semi-connector. Flashlight (Button) has raised to 300. This is a standard late-position open. My stack is very healthy (over 10,000 chips). Holding 6-2 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a button raise. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and definitely not playable even with the slight pot odds discount. Easy fold.

I am on the button with 4-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Both opponents have shown willingness to defend or re-steal. With only 13 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack, there is no reason to risk chips on such a low-equity hand. It's folded to me in the small blind, but I have J3o, which is a very weak hand even for blind-vs-blind situations. I am out of position against the Big Blind and there is no need to unnecessarily spew chips on a steal attempt with such poor equity. Folding is the optimal play.
I am dealt a very weak offsuit hand (Ten-Five offsuit) on the Button. It is not profitable to play this hand even in position, so the standard and correct play is to fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind after the Button folded. With King-high (K4o) in the Big Blind, I am facing a standard raise to 300 from Flashlight, who is in the Small Blind. Flashlight has a stack of approximately 6000 chips. I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit, which is a playable hand in a late-tournament steal position. I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. Flashlight has opened for a 3x raise from the button. Q7o is a weak hand to play out of position, especially with the chip leader (Pro) yet to act in the Big Blind. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop. Facing a standard 3 BB open from the Button. We hold ATo in the Big Blind, which is a strong hand against a Button opening range. 3-betting is an option as a bluff/value mix, but calling is often better to keep their weaker aces, broadways, and suited connectors in the pot while controlling the size of the pot since we are out of position. I'll flat call and evaluate the flop.

On this A-high flop, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the standard play. We hit top pair with a ten kicker, which is a strong hand, but we are out of position. By checking, we allow our opponent to continuation bet with their bluffs and weaker hands, keeping their range wide.

I am on the button and opened pre-flop with King-Eight offsuit. The board shows an Ace, which is a card I want to represent as the pre-flop aggressor. Since my opponent checked, they likely missed the board or have a marginal hand. A continuation bet of 300, which is slightly less than half the pot, is an effective way to put pressure on the opponent and potentially win the pot immediately.

With top pair and a decent kicker (AT) on an Ace-high board, we have a strong hand that can comfortably defend against a continuation bet. Raising here would likely fold out our opponent's bluffs, draws, and weaker made hands, while isolating us against hands that beat us like AQ, AK, sets, or two pair. Calling is the optimal play to keep Flashlight's range wide and control the pot size out of position.

The turn is the 6h, completing some straight draws (like T8 or 85) and adding two-pair possibilities, but it is largely a blank for our top pair. We still have a strong bluff-catcher, but betting out now would likely fold out our opponent's bluffs while isolating us against their stronger hands (like AK, AQ, two pairs, or sets). Checking out of position to the pre-flop raiser remains the standard line to control the pot size and allow them to continue betting with weaker hands or draws.

I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit, holding an open-ended straight draw (6-7-8-9) on a board of 7-9-A-6. My opponent, the Big Blind, checked to me on the turn. Since I already have a significant draw and a relatively modest stack, taking a free card on the turn is the most strategic move. It allows me to realize my equity and potentially hit my straight on the river without committing more chips into a pot where the opponent might have a strong Ace or a pair of 7s or 9s. Checking back is the most balanced and efficient play here.

On the river, the board pairs the Ace giving me trip Aces with a ten kicker. Since Flashlight checked behind on the turn, it's highly likely they are holding a weaker Ace, a mid-pocket pair, or a missed draw, and my hand is best. Betting for value is the clear play here to extract chips from weaker Aces or stubborn pocket pairs. A sizing of around half pot (600) is standard to get called by worse hands.

I am holding King-high on a board of 7-9-A-6-A. The opponent, Pro, checked the flop and turn, then led out for 600 on the river after the second Ace paired the board. My hand has virtually no showdown value, as any pair of 6s, 7s, 8s, or 9s, or any Ace in the opponent's range, beats me. Given that the opponent's line is either a bluff or a very thin value bet, and I have no pair to contest the pot, the most disciplined play at this stage of the tournament is to fold and preserve my remaining stack of 5725.

I am on the button with Ten-Four suited. While being suited gives it some playability, it is a very weak starting hand for a three-handed game, especially with the short-stacked Flashlight in the Big Blind who may be looking for an opportunity to shove. Pro in the Small Blind has also been quite active. I will fold this hand to maintain my chip stack and wait for a better opportunity in the final hands of the tournament.
With K7o in the small blind and the button folded, I have a decent hand to attempt a blind steal against the big blind. Raising to 300 applies pressure and capitalizes on our chip lead.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Five offsuit against the small blind's raise. Given the pot odds and my position, calling is a standard defensive play. I have 56 big blinds, so I have enough depth to see a flop and evaluate my relative hand strength, especially given the opponent's aggressive raise pattern throughout the tournament.

The flop comes out very dry and disconnected. As the pre-flop aggressor, I can represent a wide range of strong hands or overpairs here. A continuation bet of half the pot is likely to fold out my opponent's missed broadway cards or random overcards, allowing me to take down the pot without needing to improve my King-high.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high on a dry board of Nine-Six-Three. The opponent, Pro, has been aggressive and is continuing with a standard C-bet of half-pot. While I have no made hand, my Ace-high has some showdown value and I have pot odds of 3:1 to call. Given our stack depth, floating here to see if the turn improves my hand or if I can take the pot away later is a reasonable strategy against an aggressive opponent.

On the turn, the board pairs the 9, and our opponent has already called our flop continuation bet. With only King-high and no strong draws, continuing to barrel here is risky and likely to be called by any pair, decent draw, or trips. As the chip leader, checking to control the pot size and re-evaluating on the river is the most sensible play.

The opponent checked the turn after betting the flop. My hand, Ace-high, is currently a bluff catcher and has little showdown value against any made hand, but betting into the opponent only gets called by hands that beat me. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity or showdown value.

On the river, my King-high has some marginal showdown value against missed straight draws or random float hands like Queen-high or Jack-high, but it loses to Ace-high or any paired hand. Since Flashlight called the flop and checked back the turn, their range is largely weighted towards Ace-highs, small pairs, or weak floats. Bluffing now could fold out Ace-high, but checking is a much lower-risk play that controls the pot size. I will check and hope to win at showdown against a missed float, or easily fold if faced with a bet.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-high on a board of Nine-Six-Three-Nine-Four. The opponent checked the river after checking the turn. My Ace-high has showdown value against some missed bluffs or small pairs that might have checked down, but a bet here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and would only fold out hands I might beat or isolate myself against better holdings. Checking back is the standard play to take my hand to showdown.

9h 3s is an incredibly weak, unsuited, and disconnected starting hand. As the first to act pre-flop on the Button, there is no strategic value in playing this hand. Folding is the standard and correct choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with T7o. The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. The pot is 150 chips, and I have already contributed 50.
